stem: What is the dimension of the vector space of all \( [[n]] \times [[n]] \) real matrices (with the usual entrywise addition and scalar multiplication)?
solution: A basis is given by the \( [[n]]^2 \) matrices with a single \( 1 \) entry and \( 0 \) elsewhere, so the dimension is \( [[n]]^2 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts \( 2\cdot[[n]] \) (for example one entry per row and column), not all entries.
This is \( [[n]]([[n]]+1) \), the dimension counted with an extra column.
This is the dimension of \( ([[n]]+1)\times([[n]]+1) \) matrices.
This undercounts the free entries by one.
This overcounts the free entries by one.
This is the dimension of the strictly triangular part only.
This counts only an \( ([[n]]-1)\times([[n]]-1) \) block.

stem: A vector space consists of all real matrices having \( [[m]] \) rows and \( [[n]] \) columns, under the usual entrywise addition and scalar multiplication. What is its dimension?
solution: A basis is given by the \( [[m]]\cdot[[n]] \) matrices with a single \( 1 \) entry and \( 0 \) elsewhere (one for each of the \( [[m]] \) rows and \( [[n]] \) columns), so the dimension is \( [[m]]\cdot[[n]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the row and column counts instead of multiplying them.
This overcounts the free entries by one.
This undercounts the free entries by one.
This is the dimension of an \( ([[m]]+1)\times([[n]]+1) \) matrix space.
This undercounts the columns by one.
This undercounts the rows by one.
This squares both dimensions instead of just multiplying them.

stem: What is the dimension of \( P_{[[n]]} \), the vector space of all real polynomials of degree at most \( [[n]] \) (with the usual addition and scalar multiplication)?
solution: A basis is \( \{1, x, x^2, \dots, x^{[[n]]}\} \), which has \( [[n]]+1 \) elements, so \( \dim P_{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the constant term \( 1 = x^0 \), undercounting the basis by one.
This overcounts the basis by one.
This undercounts the basis by two.
This doubles the degree instead of counting monomials \( x^0,\dots,x^{[[n]]} \).
This applies the symmetric-matrix dimension formula instead of counting monomials.
This doubles the correct dimension.
This squares the degree instead of adding one to it.

stem: What is the dimension of the vector space of all symmetric \( [[n]] \times [[n]] \) real matrices (matrices \( A \) with \( A^T = A \))?
solution: A symmetric matrix is free on and above the diagonal: \( [[n]] \) diagonal entries plus \( \binom{[[n]]}{2} \) entries above it, giving \( [[n]] + \dfrac{[[n]]([[n]]-1)}{2} = \dfrac{[[n]]([[n]]+1)}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts every entry as free, ignoring the symmetry constraint.
This is the dimension of the SKEW-symmetric matrices (strictly above the diagonal only), omitting the diagonal.
This counts only the diagonal entries.
This is off by design from the diagonal count, not the full symmetric count.
This is off by one from the correct dimension.
This is off by one from the correct dimension.
This forgets to divide by \( 2 \).

stem: What is the dimension of the vector space of all skew-symmetric \( [[n]] \times [[n]] \) real matrices (matrices \( A \) with \( A^T = -A \))?
solution: Skew-symmetry forces every diagonal entry to be \( 0 \) (since \( a_{ii} = -a_{ii} \)), leaving only the entries strictly above the diagonal free: \( \binom{[[n]]}{2} = \dfrac{[[n]]([[n]]-1)}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts every entry as free, ignoring the skew-symmetry constraint.
This is the dimension of the SYMMETRIC matrices, which wrongly includes the diagonal.
This counts only the diagonal entries, which are actually forced to be \( 0 \).
This is off by design from the correct count.
This is off by one from the correct dimension.
This is off by one from the correct dimension.
This forgets to divide by \( 2 \).

stem: What is the dimension of the vector space of all traceless \( [[n]] \times [[n]] \) real matrices (matrices \( A \) with \( \operatorname{tr}(A) = 0 \))?
solution: The trace-zero condition is a single independent linear constraint (\( a_{11}+\cdots+a_{[[n]]\cdot[[n]]}=0 \)) on the \( [[n]]^2 \)-dimensional space of all matrices, cutting the dimension by exactly \( 1 \): \( [[n]]^2 - 1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This ignores the trace-zero constraint entirely.
This adds instead of subtracts the single constraint.
This drops an entire off-diagonal row's worth of entries instead of just one constraint.
This treats the constraint as removing a whole row and column.
This treats the space as one-dimensional data instead of matrices.
This is off by two from the correct dimension.
This is the dimension of a strictly larger matrix space.
